mappings.rationaleAI: Directly examines the social contract tradition from Grotius through Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau, addressing how individuals surrender natural rights for security and may reclaim sovereignty from tyrants—core to the bases of legitimacy (A.4.b), popular sovereignty (A.4.a), the state-individual relationship (A.2), and origins of constitutional government (A.5.a).

Traces the concept of sovereignty from Bodin's formal theorization through Hobbes's absolutism to revolutionary-era popular sovereignty, directly covering properties of statehood (A.1), popular sovereignty (A.4.a), social contract theory (A.4.b), and origins of constitutional government (A.5.a).

Hobbes's Leviathan argues humans must surrender freedoms to an absolute sovereign to escape the state of nature, directly addressing social contract theory (A.4.b), sovereignty (A.1), the state-individual relationship (A.2), and the concept of political power as centralized authority (C.1).

Montesquieu's theory of separation of powers into judicial, executive, and legislative branches is foundational to constitutional theory (A.5.a), describes core features of constitutional government (A.5.b), and directly addresses the tension between centralization and decentralization of power (C.4.d).

Examines the nation state as a political concept through England/Britain's evolving identity, addressing sovereignty (A.1), the nation state in the international community under globalization and European integration (A.3), nationalism (C.3.b.vii), and challenges of adapting traditional political forms to devolution and multiculturalism (C.4.e).

Explores freedom as the most basic human right spanning philosophical, theological, and political dimensions, directly relevant to the concept of human rights (C.2), the state-individual relationship (A.2), and liberal political thought on liberty versus authority (C.3.b.v).

Directly examines the doctrine that monarchs receive authority from God (A.4.d) and hereditary monarchy as venerable sanction (A.4.c), its challenge by Locke's social contract theory (A.4.b), and the intersection of church and state in political legitimation (C.4.a).

Marsilius argued that legitimate political authority derives from the people who may elect and remove rulers, an early articulation of popular sovereignty (A.4.a). His denial of papal temporal authority directly addresses church-state separation (C.4.a), and his work contributed to the intellectual origins of constitutional theory (A.5.a).

Foundational work of Western political philosophy examining forms of government from aristocracy to tyranny, proposing philosopher-kings as ideal rulers, and analyzing democracy's vulnerability to demagogues—directly addressing conceptions of legitimacy and authority (A.4), the concept of political power (C.1), and origins of constitutional theory (A.5.a).

The Gorgias examines the nature of political power through Callicles' argument that natural law supersedes convention and might makes right, directly relevant to the concept of political power (C.1) and competing conceptions of the bases of legitimacy (A.4).

Lenin invented the one-party state model and developed Marxist-Leninist ideology as a framework for communist revolution, directly instantiating communism (C.3.b.ii) and Marxism (C.3.b.vi) as political ideologies, while his seizure and exercise of power illustrates the concept of political power (C.1).

Mill's On Liberty is the canonical text of liberalism (C.3.b.v), articulating the harm principle as the sole justification for restricting individual freedom—directly addressing human rights (C.2) and the foundational tension between the state and the individual (A.2).

Directly covers anarchism as a political philosophy from Proudhon through Kropotkin, including its practical experiment during the Spanish Civil War and influence on feminism and environmentalism—the primary episode for the anarchism ideology path (C.3.b.i).

Magna Carta established rule of law, limits on royal taxation, and protections against arbitrary imprisonment—foundational to the origins of constitutional government (A.5.a), features of constitutional government such as legal restraints on power (A.5.b), the concept of human rights (C.2), and the principle of consent of the governed (A.4.a).

The trial pitted parliamentary sovereignty against divine royal authority, with Charles's execution demonstrating that a sovereign could be held accountable by the people's representatives—directly relevant to popular sovereignty (A.4.a), the challenge to divine right (A.4.d), constitutional origins (A.5.a), and constitutional change through substantive replacement (A.5.c).

Traces the British monarchy's transition from governing power to ceremonial role through constitutional crises from the Glorious Revolution to the present, directly addressing hereditary monarchy and constitutional succession (A.4.c), methods of constitutional evolution (A.5.c), and adapting traditional political forms to changing conditions (C.4.e).

Examines English republican thought from the execution of Charles I through Cromwell's Commonwealth to modern debates, directly covering popular sovereignty as the basis of government (A.4.a), origins of constitutional government (A.5.a), and constitutional change through evolution and substantive replacement (A.5.c).

The Roman Republic developed the first true constitutional government balancing consuls, senate, and popular assemblies—directly relevant to origins of constitutional government (A.5.a), features of constitutional government including institutional checks (A.5.b), and the problem of adapting political forms to changing conditions as illustrated by the Republic's collapse (C.4.e).

Solon's constitutional reforms reorganizing citizenship by wealth rather than birth and cancelling debt slavery represent an early move toward popular sovereignty (A.4.a) and a foundational moment in the origins of constitutional and democratic government (A.5.a).

The Revolution's proclamation of liberty, equality, and fraternity reshaped conceptions of popular sovereignty (A.4.a) and human rights (C.2). Burke's conservative reaction (C.3.b.iii) versus Paine's radical democratic argument (C.3.b.v) defined modern ideological fault lines, while the revolutionary transformation exemplifies constitutional change through substantive replacement (A.5.c).

mappings.episodeTitle: The French Revolution's reign of terror
mappings.rationaleAI: The Terror raises the fundamental political-theoretical question of how popular sovereignty taken to extremes produces state violence and authoritarianism (A.4.a), illustrating dangers of centralized revolutionary power (C.1, C.4.d) and the dynamics of radicalization within democratic movements.

Social Darwinism illustrates how ideological frameworks appropriating scientific ideas shaped political movements including laissez-faire economics, racial hierarchies, and fascism—directly relevant to the importance of ideology to political systems (C.3.a) and the intellectual roots of fascist ideology (C.3.b.iv).

Luxemburg's political writings on mass strike theory, critiques of Bolshevik centralism, and the relationship between democracy and socialism directly address socialism (C.3.b.viii), communism (C.3.b.ii), and Marxist political theory (C.3.b.vi) as competing ideological frameworks.

The suffrage movement's campaign for voting rights directly addresses human rights and democratic inclusion (C.2), while its diverse tactics from lobbying to civil disobedience illustrate political action by special-interest groups (B.3) and the political influence of public opinion (B.4).

The Concordat's distinction between spiritual and secular authority established a precedent for church-state separation (C.4.a) and influenced the development of limited sovereignty and the modern nation-state (A.1).

The Dreyfus Affair polarized France along lines of church, army, and republican secularism, directly contributing to the 1905 church-state separation law (C.4.a). The affair raised fundamental questions about civil liberties and justice (C.2) and demonstrated the political influence of public opinion through Zola's J'Accuse (B.4).

Freud's analysis of group psychology and the desire for domination, written against rising European fascism, directly addresses the concept of political power (C.1) and the psychological mechanisms underlying authoritarian and fascist political movements (C.3.b.iv).

William of Ockham argued that political authority derives from the governed, anticipating social contract theory and popular sovereignty (A.4.a), and challenged papal authority over secular governance, contributing to the intellectual foundations of church-state separation (C.4.a).

Analysis of the Nazi regime through interpretive frameworks of totalitarianism and the intentionalist-structuralist debate directly addresses fascism as a political ideology (C.3.b.iv) and the concept of political power in its most extreme modern manifestation (C.1).

Directly examines the future viability of the nation-state (A.1), the tension between political pragmatism and ethical principle in democratic governance as a problem of adapting traditional forms (C.4.e), and the role of media and public scrutiny in shaping democratic accountability (B.4).

The enclosure movement raises fundamental political-theoretical questions about private property versus collective rights—a core tension within the concept of human rights (C.2)—and illustrates how state legislation redistributed property and power.

American idealism rooted in Enlightenment principles of liberty, equality, and democratic self-governance directly addresses liberalism as a political ideology (C.3.b.v), the concept of human rights (C.2), and popular sovereignty as a basis of government (A.4.a).

Assessment of American global influence through the spread of democratic ideals and free-market capitalism addresses liberalism as a political ideology (C.3.b.v), while the tension between exceptionalism and hegemonic power connects to the problem of international cooperation and integration (C.4.c).

The Spanish Civil War was an ideological proxy struggle between fascism, communism, and democracy, directly illustrating fascism (C.3.b.iv) and communism (C.3.b.ii) in practice, and demonstrating the importance of ideology to political movements (C.3.a).

The aristocracy as a governing class based on hereditary titles and land ownership exemplifies venerable sanction and hereditary succession as a basis of political authority (A.4.c), while its decline through democratic reform and industrialization illustrates the problem of adapting traditional political forms to changing conditions (C.4.e).

The explosion of pamphlets and polemical literature during the English Civil War as ideological weapons between Crown and Parliament, and the emergence of early public opinion through cheap print, directly addresses the political influence of public opinion (B.4).

The Covenanters' resistance to royal imposition of episcopal governance and their formal bonds with monarchs to protect worship illustrate the church-state problem (C.4.a) and challenge divine right claims to absolute royal authority over religious matters (A.4.d).

The Han Synthesis merged Confucian ethics with cosmological frameworks into a unified state ideology, illustrating how political authority is legitimated through intellectual orthodoxy (A.4) and demonstrating the importance of ideology to a political system (C.3.a).

Zola's Germinal dramatizes tensions between capitalism, socialism, and anarchism through its depiction of miners' collective action and class struggle, serving as a literary exploration of socialism (C.3.b.viii) and anarchism (C.3.b.i) as competing ideologies.

Hill's pioneering social housing work and advocacy for reform through voluntary action rather than state intervention directly addresses the urban problem (C.4.b) and the tension between centralized state power and decentralized private initiative (C.4.d).
The play's conflict between state authority and individual conscience directly engages political theory concerning the state and the individual (A.2) and the concept of human rights (C.2).
Disraeli's One Nation Conservatism, inspired by his novel Sybil, is a founding text of modern Conservative political ideology (C.3.b.iii Conservatism).

The People's Charter and its demands for democratic reform -- equal constituencies, universal male suffrage, secret ballot -- engage theories of popular sovereignty (A.4.a continuing consent of the governed) and the concept of human rights (C.2).

Arendt theorized public action, freedom, plurality, and the vita activa against totalitarianism, engaging concepts of political power (C.1), human rights (C.2), popular sovereignty (A.4.a), and fascism as ideology (C.3.b.iv).
The play explores political legitimacy, tyranny, and the divine right of rulers (A.4.d on divine right as a basis of governmental authority).
Astell's challenge to patriarchy using the language of natural rights connects to the concept of human rights (C.2) and popular sovereignty/consent of the governed (A.4.a).
Wollstonecraft's argument for women's intellectual and political equality is a foundational text in the concept of human rights (C.2).
